elements.sent-2411-001: The Fewster-Verch (FV) framework was introduced as a prescription to define local operations within a quantum field theory (QFT) that are free from Sorkin-like causal paradoxes.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this framework the measurement device is modeled via a probe QFT that, after interacting with the target QFT, is subject to an arbitrary local measurement.
elements.sent-2411-003: While the FV framework is rich enough to carry out quantum state tomography, it has two drawbacks.
elements.sent-2411-004: First, it is unclear if the FV framework allows conducting arbitrary local measurements.
elements.sent-2411-005: Second, if the probe field is interpreted as physical and the FV framework as fundamental, then one must demand the probe measurement to be itself implementable within the framework.
elements.sent-2411-006: That would involve a new probe, which should also be subject to an FV measurement, and so on.
elements.sent-2411-007: It is unknown if there exist non-trivial FV measurements for which such an ``FV-Heisenberg cut" can be moved arbitrarily far away.
elements.sent-2411-008: In this work, we advance the first problem by proving that measurements of locally smeared fields fit within the FV framework.
elements.sent-2411-009: We solve the second problem by showing that any such field measurement admits a movable FV-Heisenberg cut.
elements.sent-2411-010: Quantum Field Theory (QFT) has proven very successful to model particle scattering experiments at high energies.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, as noted by Sorkin \cite{Sor93 , the use of seemingly innocuous quantum operations within the QFT formalism would allow three or more separate parties to violate Einstein's causality.}
elements.sent-2411-012: The accepted resolution of Sorkin's paradox is to acknowledge that the set of quantum operations which can be conducted within a finite region of space-time is certainly smaller than previously envisaged.
elements.sent-2411-013: This raises the question of how to model local operations within QFT.
elements.sent-2411-014: This problem has been greatly advanced in recent years.
elements.sent-2411-015: In \cite{Jub22 , Jubb studies which quantum channels (or update rules) do not lead to Sorkin-type violations of causality in QFT, a property that he calls strong causality.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Among other things, he concludes that weak ``Gaussian'' measurements of locally smeared fields are strongly causal.
elements.sent-2411-017: Oeckl, who calls this property causal transparency, reaches the same conclusion in \cite{Oec24a .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Independently, Fewster and Verch proposed in \cite{FV20,FV23 a general framework to model measurements in QFT.}
elements.sent-2411-019: In a nutshell, the FV framework consists in making the `target' field theory interact in a compact region with another `pointer' or `probe' field theory.
elements.sent-2411-020: Measurement outcomes are obtained by measuring the pointer in an arbitrary bounded region outside the causal past of the interaction region.
elements.sent-2411-021: The pointer is then discarded, i.e., one is not allowed to measure it a second time.
elements.sent-2411-022: Remarkably, all quantum operations achievable within the FV framework are strongly causal \cite{BFR21 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Moreover, they suffice to carry out full tomography when the target QFT is a scalar field \cite{FJR23 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: The FV framework has some drawbacks, though:.
elements.sent-2411-025: Given the specification of a quantum measurement, or even the description of the corresponding quantum instrument, it is not straightforward to determine whether the FV framework allows implementing one or the other---even approximately.
elements.sent-2411-026: Doing so involves specifying a probe QFT, its initial state, the form of the interaction and the measurement on the probe, or proving that no such elements exist.
elements.sent-2411-027: If we regard the probe QFT as a physical entity and not as part of a mere mathematical construction to obtain a set of strongly causal operations, then it is unclear how come that one is allowed to measure the probe arbitrarily; a standing assumption of the framework.
elements.sent-2411-028: Note that, if the probe does not magically disappear at the end of the measurement, then allowing arbitrary sequential measurements thereof would run again into Sorkin's paradox.
elements.sent-2411-029: Consistency therefore demands that the probe measurement itself be modeled within the FV framework.
elements.sent-2411-030: That would involve introducing another probe, whose measurement should be realizable within the FV framework, and so on.
elements.sent-2411-031: In other words, any physical measurement should be expressible as an arbitrarily long chain of consecutive interactions with independent probes, in such a way that we predict the same measurement statistics irrespective of where in the chain we invoke Born's rule.
elements.sent-2411-032: In non-relativistic quantum mechanics, the specific probe or pointer within a measurement chain where one applies the collapse postulate is known as ``Heisenberg cut".
elements.sent-2411-033: That the position of the cut can be chosen at will was already observed by von Neumann \cite{Neu55 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: What we have argued above is that, if the FV framework is fundamental and its probe field is meant to be physical, then feasible QFT measurements should have a similarly movable ``FV-Heisenberg cut".
elements.sent-2411-035: Which brings us to the question: are there non-trivial FV-realizable measurements with a movable FV-Heisenberg cut?.
elements.sent-2411-036: In this paper, we address the first problem by proving that Gaussian-modulated measurements of a locally smeared quantum field can be modeled within the FV framework.
elements.sent-2411-037: As it turns out, the probe measurement that induces a Gaussian-modulated field measurement can itself be chosen a Gaussian-modulated field measurement.
elements.sent-2411-038: This solves the second problem: Gaussian field measurements are consistent with the FV framework, even if we accept the physicality of the probe field.
elements.sent-2411-039: As a byproduct, we establish that also projective field measurements can be modeled within the FV framework.
elements.sent-2411-040: The structure of this paper is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-041: In \Cref{sec:quantumops we introduce how to model general as well as Gaussian-modulated measurements in QFT, distinguishing between positive-operator-valued measures (POVMs) and quantum instruments.}
elements.sent-2411-042: We also specify the linear QFT setting to which our results apply.
elements.sent-2411-043: In \Cref{sec:fv we summarize the FV framework and define FV-realizable POVMs and instruments.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Finally, in \Cref{sec:impl we prove our results by presenting a concrete scheme to induce Gaussian-modulated measurements, projective measurements and more---even iteratively.}
elements.sent-2411-045: We will be dealing with the standard framework of AQFT: namely, starting from a fixed globally hyperbolic spacetime manifold $\M$ we associate to any region $R\subset \M$ a unital *-algebra $\A(R)$.
elements.sent-2411-046: These local algebras, which define our QFT, satisfy the usual axioms: isotony, microcausality, time-slice and Haag's property; confer \cite[Sec.~4]{FV23 and references therein for details.}
elements.sent-2411-047: States of this QFT are linear, positive functionals $\omega:\A\to\CC$ on the global algebra $\A \defby \A(\M)$ with $\omega(1)=1$.
elements.sent-2411-048: An element $a \in \A$ is said to be localizable in region $R\subset \M$ if $a\in\A(R)$.
elements.sent-2411-049: To express limits, we assume $\A$ and all $\A(R)$ are equipped and completed with respect to a suitable topology.
elements.sent-2411-050: Specifically, we work with von-Neumann algebras; for some fixed Hilbert space $\H$ these are *-subalgebras of $\mc{B (\H)$ closed under the strong-operator topology.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Limits, infinite sums and integrals pertaining to $\A$ will be taken with respect to this topology.
elements.sent-2411-052: Note that in this setting any density matrix, i.e., $\rho \in \mc{B (\H)$ such that $\rho \geq 0$ and $\operatorname{tr \rho = 1$, gives rise to a state $\omega_\rho$ on $\A$ via $\omega_\rho(a) \defby \operatorname{tr (\rho a)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: We wish to model local quantum operations and instruments within this QFT.
elements.sent-2411-054: A quantum instrument is defined by a family of completely positive maps $\Omega \defby \{ \Omega_b \}_{b\in \mc{B }$, where $\Omega_b:\A\to\A$ is such that \begin{equation}\label{eq:sum1} \bar{\Omega}(a) \defby \sum_b \Omega_b(a) \quad\text{satisfies}\quad \bar{\Omega}(1) = 1;\end{equation} assuming for the moment that the index set $\mc{B}$ is finite to avoid technicalities.}
elements.sent-2411-055: The map $\bar{\Omega : \A \to \A$ is called the measurement channel.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Intuitively, if our QFT is initially in state $\omega$, the instrument $\{\Omega_b \}_b$ describes a measurement that returns an outcome $b$ with probability \begin{equation}p(b|\omega)=\omega(\Omega_b(1)),\end{equation} in which case the new state of the QFT is updated to \begin{equation}\bar{\omega}_b \defby \frac{1}{\omega(\Omega_b(1))}\omega\circ\Omega_b.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-057: If we ignore or discard the measurement outcome $b$, then the new QFT state will be \begin{equation}\label{eq:sum2} \bar{\omega} \defby \sum_bp(b|\omega)\bar{\omega}_b=\omega\circ\bar{\Omega}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-058: If we are just interested in computing the probabilities of the different outcomes $b$, it suffices to work with the algebra elements \begin{equation}M_b \defby \Omega_b(1).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-060: Note that the elements $M \defby \{ M_b \}_b$ satisfy \be \sum_b M_b=1 \quad \text{and}\quad \forall b: \, M_b\geq 0.
elements.sent-2411-061: The formalism above can also be used to model measurements with infinitely many outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-062: In that case, we distinguish whether $\mc{B}$ is discrete or continuous.
elements.sent-2411-063: In the discrete case, we assume that $ \Omega_b(a)$ is summable in $b$ for all $a \in \A$ which implies the existence of the measurement channel $\overline{\Omega (a) \defby \sum_{b \in \mc{B \Omega_b(a)$ as a limit of partial sums.}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: In the continuous case, we suppose the set of outcomes $\mc{B}$ to be a Borel set within a standard Borel space, e.g. an interval on the real line, and that $\Omega_b(a)$ is integrable in $b$ for all $a \in \A$.

elements.sent-2411-115: Recently, Fewster and Verch proposed a set quantum instruments that are consistent with Einstein's causality and moreover sufficiently general to include measurements \cite{FV20,FV23 .}
elements.sent-2411-116: Their main idea was to model the measurement of a `target' QFT observable by making it interact with a `pointer' or `probe' QFT in a region $K$.
elements.sent-2411-117: The probe field is measured using a POVM with elements localized in some processing region $L$ strictly separated from the past of $K$.
elements.sent-2411-118: After discarding (`tracing out') the probe field, the resulting quantum instrument induced in the target theory is shown to be localizable in any causally complete region strictly containing $K$.
elements.sent-2411-119: Moreover, the composition of such instruments is compatible with Einstein's causality \cite{BFR21 .}

elements.sent-2411-121: The coupling between system and probe is specified in terms of an automorphism $\Theta$ acting on the uncoupled theory $\mc{S}\otimes \mc{P}$ implementing the interaction---referred to as the scattering morphism.

elements.sent-2411-302: The Fewster-Verch (FV) framework provides a local and covariant approach for defining measurements in quantum field theory (QFT).
elements.sent-2411-303: Within this framework, a probe QFT represents the measurement device, which, after interacting with the target QFT, undergoes an arbitrary local measurement.
elements.sent-2411-304: Remarkably, the FV framework is free from Sorkin-like causal paradoxes and robust enough to enable quantum state tomography.
elements.sent-2411-306: In this work, we advance the first problem by proving that Gaussian-modulated measurements of locally smeared fields fit within the FV framework.
elements.sent-2411-307: We solve the second problem by showing that any such measurement admits a movable FV-Heisenberg cut.
elements.sent-2411-308: As a technical byproduct, we establish that state transformations induced by finite-rank perturbations of the classical phase space underlying a linear scalar field preserve the Hadamard property.
elements.sent-2411-309: Quantum Field Theory (QFT) has proven very successful in modeling particle scattering experiments at high energies.
elements.sent-2411-310: The accepted resolution of Sorkin's paradox is to acknowledge that the set of quantum operations which can be conducted within a finite region of space-time is smaller than previously envisaged.
elements.sent-2411-311: In \cite{Jub22 , Jubb studies which quantum channels (or update rules) do not lead to Sorkin-type violations of causality in QFT, a property he calls strong causality.}
elements.sent-2411-312: Oeckl, who calls this property causal transparency, reaches the same conclusion in \cite{Oec24 .}
elements.sent-2411-313: Independently, Fewster and Verch proposed in \cite{FV20,FV23 a general framework to model measurements within QFT.}
elements.sent-2411-314: In a nutshell, the FV framework consists in making the `target' (or `system') field theory interact in a compact region with another `probe' field theory.
elements.sent-2411-315: Measurement outcomes are obtained by measuring the probe in an arbitrary bounded region outside the causal past of the interaction region.
elements.sent-2411-316: The probe is then discarded, i.e., one is not allowed to measure it a second time.
elements.sent-2411-317: However, as we argue later on, this is different from modeling actual measurements.
elements.sent-2411-318: In this regard, the FV framework presents at least two unresolved issues:.
elements.sent-2411-319: First, given the specification of a quantum measurement, or even of the corresponding quantum instrument, it is not straightforward to determine whether the FV framework allows implementing one or the other---even approximately.
elements.sent-2411-320: Doing so involves specifying a probe QFT, its initial state, the form of the interaction, and the measurement on the probe, or proving that no such elements exist.
elements.sent-2411-321: Second, if we regard the probe QFT as a physical entity and not as part of a mere mathematical construction to obtain a set of strongly causal operations, then it is unclear how come one is allowed to measure the probe arbitrarily; a standing assumption of the framework.
elements.sent-2411-322: In non-relativistic quantum mechanics, the specific probe within a measurement chain where one applies Born's rule is known as ``Heisenberg cut".
elements.sent-2411-323: That the position of the cut can be chosen at will was already observed by von Neumann \cite{Von96 .}
elements.sent-2411-324: This leads us to ask: Are there non-trivial FV-realizable measurements with a movable FV-Heisenberg cut?.
elements.sent-2411-325: As a byproduct, we establish that projective field measurements can also be modeled within the FV framework.
elements.sent-2411-326: In \Cref{sec:alg we specify the linear QFT setting to which our results apply.}
elements.sent-2411-327: In \Cref{sec:quantumops we introduce how to model general as well as Gaussian-modulated measurements in QFT, distinguishing between positive-operator-valued measures (POVMs) and quantum instruments, and illustrate how this differs from state tomography.}
elements.sent-2411-328: In \Cref{sec:fv we summarize the FV framework and characterize the POVMs and instruments that are realizable within the framework in order to state our main results (\Cref{thm:asymptFV ).}}
elements.sent-2411-329: Finally, in \Cref{sec:impl we prove our results by presenting a concrete scheme to induce Gaussian-modulated measurements, projective measurements, and more---even iteratively.}
elements.sent-2411-331: In Appendix~\ref{app:aqft we review the construction of the Weyl CCR algebra of a linear scalar field.}
elements.sent-2411-332: In Appendix~\ref{app:finrank , we establish that state transformations induced by finite-rank perturbations of the classical phase space underlying a linear scalar field preserve the Hadamard property.}
elements.sent-2411-333: In Appendix~\ref{app:gauss we collect some proofs on (dephased) Gaussian instruments.}
elements.section-2411-009: Technical setting for quantum field theory
elements.sent-2411-334: We will be dealing with the standard framework of AQFT: namely, starting from a fixed globally hyperbolic spacetime manifold $\M$, we associate to any region $\Reg\subset \M$ a unital *-algebra $\A(\Reg)$.
elements.sent-2411-335: States of this QFT are linear, positive functionals $\omega:\A\to\C$ on the algebra of observables $\A \defby \A(\M)$ with $\omega(1)=1$.
elements.sent-2411-336: An element $A \in \A$ is said to be localizable in region $\Reg\subset \M$ if $\a\in\A(\Reg)$.
elements.sent-2411-337: To express limits, we assume $\A$ and all $\A(\Reg)$ are equipped and completed with respect to a suitable topology.
elements.sent-2411-338: Specifically, we work with von-Neumann algebras; for some fixed Hilbert space $\H$ these are *-subalgebras of $B(\H)$ closed under the strong-operator topology\footnote{..., or closed under the weak-operator-topology, a more common but equivalent definition of a von-Neumman algebra.. .}
elements.sent-2411-339: Limits, infinite sums, and integrals pertaining to $\A$ will be taken with respect to this topology, with integrals understood as Bochner integrals in this topology.
elements.sent-2411-340: Note that in this setting, any density matrix, i.e., $\rho \in B(\H)$ such that $\rho \geq 0$ and $\operatorname{tr \rho = 1$, gives rise to a state $\omega_\rho$ on $\A$ through $\omega_\rho(\a ) \defby \operatorname{tr (\rho \a )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-341: Our main study will be conducted in the setting of linear scalar QFT.
elements.sent-2411-342: In this setting the smeared field $\Phi(f)$ corresponds to the quantization of \begin{equation}\phi(f) = \int_\M \phi(x) f(x) \mathrm{vol}(x) =: \braket{\phi,f},\end{equation} a classical point field $\phi$ satisfying a classical linear differential equation $T\phi(x)= 0$ and smeared over spacetime through a test function $f$ using $\mathrm{vol}$, the volume form\footnote{Its dependence on the spacetime metric is suppressed from our notation for $\M$ since it is considered to be fixed throughout the article.. on $\M$.}
elements.sent-2411-343: We assume that $T$ is symmetric with respect to $\braket{,}$ and normally hyperbolic, which implies it has well-defined retarded/advanced Green operators $E^\pm$ and associated causal propagator $E= E^--E^+$ \cite{BGP07 .}
elements.sent-2411-344: This holds, e.g., for the Klein-Gordon operator $T = \square_\M - m^2$, $m > 0$.
elements.sent-2411-345: We denote the space of real-valued smooth compactly supported functions on $\M$ by $\SmoothC(\M)$ and by $\SmoothC(\Reg)$ if restricting to functions having support in $\Reg \subset \M$.
elements.sent-2411-346: With $\phi$ having spatially compact support, it is natural to identify $Tf \sim 0$ for all $f \in \SmoothC(\M)$ since $\phi(Tf) = \braket{T\phi,f = 0$ by partial integration.}
elements.sent-2411-347: We denote the resulting quotient space by $\mc{C _T \defby \SmoothC(\M)/T\SmoothC(\M)$ and say that $f \in \SmoothC(\M)$ is localizable in $\Reg \subset \M$ iff there exists $g \in \SmoothC(\Reg)$ such that $f \sim g$.}
elements.sent-2411-349: The classical timeslice property \cite[Thm.~3.3.1]{BDFY15 entails that any $f\in \SmoothC(\M)$ is localizable in any globally hyperbolic neighbourhood $N$ of any Cauchy surface of $M$.}
elements.sent-2411-350: We define the classical phase space as the symplectic space $(\mc{C _T,E)$, where $E$ denotes also the symplectic form $E([f],[g]) \defby \braket{f, Eg}$, $f,g \in \SmoothC(\M)$ , associated with the causal propagator.}
elements.sent-2411-351: This concludes the classical part of the construction.
elements.sent-2411-352: Quantization may then be performed in a fully algebraic setting which we allude to in Appendix~\ref{app:aqft .}
elements.sent-2411-353: For the main text, however, we are content with a fixed Hilbert space representation and make these assumptions: The smeared field $\Phi$ is a map from $\mc{C _T$ to self-adjoint operators on a Hilbert space $\H$ such that \begin{equation}\label{eq:cont} [Continuity] \, e^{it\Phi(f)}  is strongly operator continuous in t, \label{eq:weyl} [Weyl relation] \, e^{i\Phi(f)} e^{i\Phi(g)} = e^{-\frac{i}{2} \braket{f,Eg}} e^{i\Phi(f+g)},\end{equation} for all $f,g \in \SmoothC(\M)$, where we agree to $\Phi(f)\defby \Phi([f])$ by abuse of notation.}
elements.sent-2411-354: Finally, we obtain a net of von-Neumann algebras $\{ \A(\Reg)\}_\Reg \subset B(\H)$ labeled by open causally convex regions $\Reg \subset \M$ by defining $\A(\Reg)$ to be the algebra generated by the Weyl operators $\{ e^{i\Phi(f) : \, f \in \SmoothC(\Reg)\}$, equipped and completed with respect to the strong operator topology of $B(\H)$.}
elements.sent-2411-355: We will call this a linear scalar QFT (induced by $T$) and denote it by $\A_T$.
elements.sent-2411-356: We conclude this section by specifying suitable states and state transformations on $\A_T$.
elements.sent-2411-357: For QFTs (at least for linear ones) there is a broad class of physically reasonable states given by the quasi-free Hadamard states.
elements.sent-2411-358: There are many such states on any globally hyperbolic spacetime \cite{Ver94 and for stationary spacetimes this includes ground and thermal states \cite{San13 .}}
elements.sent-2411-359: There is also a broad class of state transformations: Any symplectic transformation $F$ of $(\mc{C _T,E)$ induces an automorphism $\alpha_F$ of $\A_T$ by imposing $\alpha_F(e^{i\Phi(v) ) \defby e^{i\Phi(Fv)}$, $v \in \mc{C _T$ and a new $F$-transformed state $\omega_F$ via pullback, i.e., \begin{equation}\label{eq:transformedstate} \omega_F(\bullet) \defby \omega(\alpha_F(\bullet)).\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-360: To deem a transformation of the form \begin{equation}\label{eq:statetrafo} \omega \mapsto \omega_F\end{equation} as `physical', e.g., arising as a state-update for a localized measurement, one would at least require that it preserves the class of physically reasonable states.
elements.sent-2411-361: It is well known (confer, e.g., \cite[Prop.~29.3-2]{HR15 ) that the class of quasi-free states is preserved by all transformations which have the form \eqref{eq:statetrafo .}}
elements.sent-2411-362: In Appendix~\ref{app:finrank we prove that these preserve also the Hadamard class if $F$ transforms only a finite-dimensional subspace of $\mc{C _T$.}}
elements.sent-2411-363: Although possibly known to experts, this result, to the best of our knowledge, has not been addressed in the existing literature and might be of independent interest.
elements.sent-2411-364: Since our main study does not require to work with Hadamard states, we will only define quasi-free states in more detail: A state $\omega$ on $\A_T$ is referred to as quasi-free (sometimes also as ``Gaussian'') iff there exists a positive bilinear symmetric form $\Gamma:\mc{C _T^{\times 2}\to \R$ such that \begin{equation}\label{eq:qfree} \omega(e^{i\Phi(v)})=e^{-\frac{\Gamma(v,v)}{2}}, \quad v \in \mc{C}_T.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-365: Conversely, \eqref{eq:qfree defines a state on $\A_T$ iff \begin{equation}\label{eq:uncertaintyrel} \Gamma(v,v) \Gamma(w,w) \geq \tfrac{1}{4} E(v,w)^2, \quad v,w \in \mc{C}_T.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-366: We refer to such $\Gamma$ as a covariance.
elements.sent-2411-367: Given any symplectic transformation $F$ of $(\mc{C _T,E)$, it is straightforward to check that the $F$-transformed state $\omega_F$ is also quasi-free with covariance \begin{equation}\Gamma_F(v,w) \defby \Gamma(Fv,Fw), \quad v,w \in \mc{C}_T.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-368: A quasi-free state is called pure iff \eqref{eq:uncertaintyrel can be saturated, i.e., for any given nonzero $v \in \mc{C _T$, \begin{equation}\label{eq:optuncertainty} \underset{0\neq w \in \mc{C}_T}{\sup}\, \frac{E(v,w)^2}{4 \Gamma(v,v)\Gamma(w,w)} = 1.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-369: Note that also pure quasi-free Hadamard states exist in arbitrary globally hyperbolic spacetimes.
elements.sent-2411-370: In stationary spacetimes, these exist since, at least for a free scalar field with a positive stationary potential, i.e., where $T= \square_\M + V$ with stationary $0 < V \in C^\infty(\M)$, there is a unique (pure) ground state \cite{San13 .}
elements.sent-2411-371: More generally, existence follows by a deformation argument\footnote{We thank Chris~Fewster for the helpful suggestion.}
elements.sent-2411-372: A quasi-free Hadamard state on $\M$ may then be constructed by taking suitable extensions and restrictions of a pure quasi-free Hadamard state on the static patch of $\M'$.
elements.sent-2411-373: That this state is also pure follows using the defining relation \eqref{eq:optuncertainty and the classical timeslice property.}
elements.sent-2411-374: We wish to model local operations within QFT, specifically measurements and associated instruments.
elements.sent-2411-375: In particular, we will use the technical setting from the previous section and fix some QFT with algebra of observables $\A$.
elements.sent-2411-376: Later on we illustrate these notions defining Gaussian operations in a linear scalar QFT.
elements.sent-2411-377: Generally, a (quantum) measurement comes with a set of (classical) measurement outcomes $\Out$ and associates with it a (classical) probability distribution which depends on the state of the (quantum) system---in our case a QFT---before the measurement.
elements.sent-2411-378: If we add a description of the state of the system after the measurement, we speak of a (quantum) instrument.
elements.sent-2411-379: To avoid technicalities, we treat the two paradigmatic cases of a finite- and a continuous-outcome measurement and refer to \cite{BLPY16 for generalizations.}
elements.sent-2411-380: Moreover, as it is convenient, we define our instruments in the Heisenberg picture acting directly on $\A$---confer e.g. \cite{Wer01,Key02 ---and provide the associated state changes in form of state update rules.}
elements.sent-2411-381: To begin with, let $\Out$ be finite.
elements.sent-2411-382: In this case, an instrument is defined by a family of linear completely positive maps $\Instr \defby \{ \Instr_j \}_{j\in \Out}$, where $\Instr_j :\A\to\A$ is such that \begin{equation}\label{eq:sum1} \bar{\Instr}(\a ) \defby \sum_j \Instr_j (\a ) \quad\text{satisfies}\quad \bar{\Instr}(1) = 1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-383: The map $\bar{\Instr : \A \to \A$ is called the measurement channel.}
elements.sent-2411-384: Intuitively, if our QFT is initially in state $\omega$, the instrument $\Instr$ describes a measurement that returns an outcome $j$ with probability \begin{equation}\label{prob:1} p(j |\omega)=\omega(\Instr_j (1)),\end{equation} in which case the new state of the QFT is updated to \begin{equation}\bar{\omega}_j \defby \frac{1}{\omega(\Instr_j (1))}\omega\circ\Instr_j .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-385: If we ignore or discard the measurement outcome $j$, then the new QFT state will be \begin{equation}\label{eq:sum2} \bar{\omega} \defby \sum_j p(j |\omega)\bar{\omega}_j =\omega\circ\bar{\Instr}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-386: If we are just interested in computing the probabilities of the different outcomes, it suffices to work with the algebra elements $M \defby \{ M_j \defby I_j(1) \}_j$ so that $p(j |\omega)=\omega(M_j )$.
elements.sent-2411-387: These elements satisfy \begin{equation}\label{eq:sum3} \sum_j M_j =1 \quad \text{and}\quad \forall j : \, M_j \geq 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-388: Any such family $M$ is called a positive operator-valued measure (POVM).
elements.sent-2411-389: The formalism above can also be used to model continuous-outcome measurements.
elements.sent-2411-390: In this case we suppose the set of outcomes $\Out \subset \R$ to be a Borel subset of the real line, e.g., an interval or the full real line, we label the outcomes with indices $\out$ instead of $j$ for distinction\footnote{In a few cases we also use $\alpha$ as index if we simultaneously treat finite and continuous outcomes.}
elements.sent-2411-391: Further, we require that $\Instr_\out (\a)$ is integrable in $\out $ for all $\a \in \A$.
elements.sent-2411-392: Here, "integrable" refers to Bochner integrability with respect to the strong-operator topology (confer Appendix~\ref{app:gauss for a definition).}
elements.sent-2411-393: Note that this assumes more than strictly necessary to keep the statistical interpretation from above, Eqs.~\eqref{prob:1 --\eqref{eq:sum2 , which relies only on the existence of limits within states; for example, the weak-operator topology would be sufficient in this regard.}}

elements.sent-2411-395: Now, we are ready to discuss concrete examples of instruments and POVMs.
elements.sent-2411-396: In order to do this, we take $\A$ to be a linear scalar QFT as specified in the preceding section (dropping the index $T$) and denote the associated quantized field by $\Phi$.
elements.sent-2411-397: We remark that expressions of the form $\mu(\Phi(f))$ for any bounded Borel function $\mu:\R \to \mathbbm{C}$, by means of the spectral theorem, define elements of $\A \subset B(\H)$ in terms of (Borel) functional calculus.
elements.sent-2411-398: From this perspective, most statements made in the remainder of this section are direct consequences of the properties of the functions $\mu$, and we defer proofs to Appendix~\ref{app:gauss .}
elements.sent-2411-399: To start with, let $f\in \SmoothC(\M)$ and $\epsilon>0$, and consider the following continuous-outcome POVM on $\A$, \begin{equation}M^{f,\epsilon} \defby \{ M_\out^{f,\epsilon}\}_{\out \in\R}, \quad M_\out^{f,\epsilon} \defby \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\epsilon} e^{-\frac{(\Phi(f)-\out )^2}{2\epsilon^2}}. \label{gaussian_POVM}\end{equation} We will call this POVM a Gaussian-modulated measurement of the field $\Phi(f)$, or Gaussian measurement, for short.

elements.sent-2411-401: Moreover, it approximates a projective measurement of $\Phi(f)$ in the limit $\epsilon \to 0$, i.e., for any Borel set $B \subset \R$ and with $\Pi_B(A)$ denoting the spectral projection of a self-adjoint operator $A$ associated with the spectral subset $B$, \begin{equation}\label{eq:gaussPOVMlimit} \Pi_B(\Phi(f))=\underset{\epsilon\to 0}{\lim} \, \int_B M_\out^{f,\epsilon} d\out .\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-429: More formally, call $\S$ the target QFT and $\P$ the probe QFT, which we assume to be in state $\sigma$.

elements.section-2411-013: Preservation of Hadamard condition under finite-rank perturbations
elements.sent-2411-584: In this section we show that the class of Hadamard states---and the subclass of quasi-free Hadamard states---of a real linear scalar field on a globally hyperbolic spacetime are preserved under finite-rank perturbations of the underlying classical phase space.
elements.sent-2411-585: While the result presented here might be known to experts, it has to the best of the author's knowledge not been addressed in the literature\footnote{Note though \cite[Thm.~C.4]{Ver94 which gives a similar argument using \cite[App.~B]{KW91 and which implies the result for the Klein-Gordon field under single-rank perturbations.. .}}}
elements.sent-2411-586: However, this class of transformations, which we call finite-rank perturbations for short, is very relevant in applications and arise for instance in the context of quantum optics whose instruments typically act only on a finite number of field modes (at least in idealization).
elements.sent-2411-587: The proof relies on the fact that such symplectic transformations induce smooth changes of the two-point function.
